title: Why tracking market structure is safer than following the news cycle
author: Disastrous_Hotel_574
selftext:
Hi everyone, as a developer, I’ve learned that when the global economy feels unpredictable, the only thing you can actually rely on is clean data and a strict execution plan. I built to help me filter out the noise and focus on objective price action.

I use a stack of Next.js and Supabase to ensure that my performance tracking is handled by a real database instead of a manual spreadsheet that might lag during high-volatility events.

Here is how this approach helped me navigate the charts this morning:

* Systematic Logic: My AI analyzer identified a high-probability structure on Gold, allowing me to ignore the conflicting economic headlines.
* Execution: I closed a trade with a 2.75 RR, resulting in a 150 profit. The dashboard kept me in the trade by showing me the mathematical validity of the setup.
* Data Integrity: The gain was instantly logged in PostgreSQL, updating my XIRR and drawdown metrics automatically.
* Risk Control: By hardcoding my risk parameters into the terminal, I ensure that my survival isn't dependent on "gut feeling" or market luck.

The tool is in free beta for anyone who wants to stop guessing and start trading with engineering-level discipline. Link is at the top.

Are you guys still relying on news alerts for your entries, or have you moved to a more data-centric infrastructure? 📈💻

comments.author: dogazine4570
comments.body:
I like this mindset. News is seductive because it *feels* actionable, but most of the time it just explains what already happened on the chart.

Focusing on structure (HH/HL, LH/LL, liquidity sweeps, range breaks, etc.) gives you predefined invalidation levels. That alone reduces emotional decision-making. When CPI or FOMC hits, volatility expands—but if you already know your levels, you’re reacting to structure, not headlines.

I’m also a fan of tracking performance in an actual database instead of spreadsheets. Once you can tag trades by setup type, market condition, session, etc., patterns in your own behavior become obvious. That feedback loop is more valuable than any macro narrative.

Curious—are you using your stack just for journaling, or are you also pulling historical price data to backtest structural setups automatically?

replies.author: Disastrous_Hotel_574
replies.body:
Exactly. News is just the catalyst; structure is the roadmap.

I built [https://herculmarket.com](https://herculmarket.com) to bridge that gap between journaling and raw data analysis. To answer your question:

* It’s more than just a journal; the backend calculates real-time performance metrics like XIRR and drawdown directly via PostgreSQL.
* The AI analyzer helps identify liquidity sweeps and BOS automatically, so my invalidation levels are based on cold math rather than a seductive headline.
* The goal is to move toward pulling more historical data to refine the feedback loop you mentioned, making it easier to tag and isolate high-probability regimes.

The feedback loop is where the edge lives. If you want to check out how the data stack handles these structure shifts, the beta is free.
